target.title: [security] Brokered artifact uploads omit org from object namespace
target.body:
# [security] Brokered artifact uploads omit org from object namespace

## Summary

Brokered artifact upload grants are scoped by the authenticated `owner` value and caller-controlled artifact prefix, but not by the authenticated `org`. GitHub browser-login user tokens carry both `owner` and `org`, and Crabbox's maintainer-authored security guidance describes user-token access as owner/org-scoped for coordinator resources. However, `POST /v1/artifacts/uploads` drops `org` before deriving object-store keys, so the same GitHub user authorized in two allowed orgs on one coordinator can mint upload and read grants for the same artifact object namespace from either org token.

This crosses an in-scope Crabbox boundary: it is a coordinator-mediated, authenticated resource access issue, not trusted repository automation, same-OS-user exposure, or a trusted operator deliberately executing commands on their own lease.

## Affected Components

- Checked commit: `5001cd1179a42c174da65c8e8a87bf16218aef95`
- Component: coordinator brokered artifact publishing
- Maintainer policy evidence:
  - `SECURITY.md` source: `maintainer-authored target security policy`
  - `SECURITY.md` git blob: `b89a403647e11891375eb9765121348a22bcc362`
  - `SECURITY.md` SHA-256: `43cfd2f02d69e7febedd509695f498ae77fd9be1e2d84c8c2e9e8f504d63a85a`
- Maintainer operations evidence:
  - `docs/security.md` source: `maintainer-authored target document`
  - `docs/security.md` git blob: `c481406ef920acb757f271880f87eb830741618d`
  - `docs/security.md` SHA-256: `c3ae6608a50f6933d30c76b64c956079a4610721318a35d438b9c068c7384f89`
- Relevant source:
  - `worker/src/coordinator-entry.ts:86-106`
  - `worker/src/auth.ts:30-39`
  - `worker/src/auth.ts:90-100`
  - `worker/src/auth.ts:126-146`
  - `worker/src/fleet.ts:855-856`
  - `worker/src/fleet.ts:9046-9050`
  - `worker/src/http.ts:28-30`
  - `worker/src/usage.ts:75-83`
  - `worker/src/artifacts.ts:57-68`
  - `worker/src/artifacts.ts:191-201`
  - `worker/src/artifacts.ts:223-228`
  - `worker/src/oauth.ts:428-495`
  - `internal/cli/artifacts_publish.go:203-252`
  - `internal/cli/artifacts_publish.go:255-272`
  - `worker/test/fleet.test.ts:16289-16348`

## Attack Path

Attacker role: authenticated GitHub browser-login user.

Prerequisites:

- The coordinator enables brokered artifact publishing with `CRABBOX_ARTIFACTS_*` storage credentials.
- The same GitHub user is authorized in two allowed orgs on the same coordinator, resulting in two signed user tokens with the same `owner` and different `org` values.
- The attacker knows or can predict a target artifact prefix and file name. Default hosted publishing prefixes include PR number, bundle directory name, and timestamp; explicit prefixes remain caller-controlled, and generated Markdown or manifests can expose final object URLs.

Steps:

1. A victim publishes an artifact while authenticated for org A. The object key is derived as `CRABBOX_ARTIFACTS_PREFIX/<owner>/<prefix>/<name>`.
2. The attacker authenticates as the same GitHub user for org B and sends `POST /v1/artifacts/uploads` with the same `prefix` and `files[].name`.
3. `prepareCoordinatorRequest` authenticates the request and injects both `x-crabbox-owner` and `x-crabbox-org`, but `createArtifactUploads` passes only `requestOwner(request)` to `artifactUploadResponse`.
4. `artifactUploadResponse` calls `artifactPrefix(config.prefix, owner, request.prefix)`, so `org` never becomes part of the object namespace.
5. The coordinator returns a signed PUT grant and either a public final URL or signed read URL for the same object key used by org A.
6. The org B-authenticated caller can overwrite that object during the PUT grant window, or read it when the returned URL grants access to an existing private object.

The source-level dataflow is:

```text
GitHub user token for org B
  -> authenticateRequest verifies payload.owner and payload.org
  -> requestWithAuthContext sets x-crabbox-owner and x-crabbox-org
  -> POST /v1/artifacts/uploads
  -> createArtifactUploads reads requestOwner(request) only
  -> artifactPrefix(configPrefix, owner, requestPrefix)
  -> signed PUT/GET or public final URL for configPrefix/owner/requestPrefix/name
```

## Impact

Brokered artifacts are intended to keep object-store credentials server-side while allowing authenticated Crabbox users to publish screenshots, logs, manifests, and QA proof bundles. Because the key namespace omits `org`, org-scoped authorization does not separate brokered artifact objects for a user who belongs to multiple allowed orgs on one coordinator.

The practical impact is bounded but security-relevant: an authenticated user can replace or retrieve known artifact objects for the same owner across orgs. This can disclose proof files, screenshots, logs, manifests, or other published evidence, and can corrupt PR or audit evidence by replacing a known object key with attacker-controlled content. The finding is limited to brokered artifact data for the same authenticated owner; it does not grant admin capability or arbitrary cross-owner access.

## Severity Assessment

### CVSS Assessment

| Metric | v3.1 | v4.0 |
|--------|------|------|
| **Score** | 5.4 / 10.0 | 5.3 / 10.0 |
| **Severity** | Medium | Medium |
| **Vector** | `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N` | `CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:L/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N` |
| **Calculator** | [CVSS v3.1 Calculator](https://www.first.org/cvss/calculator/3.1#CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N) | [CVSS v4.0 Calculator](https://www.first.org/cvss/calculator/4.0#CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:L/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N) |

The score is Medium because exploitation is network-reachable after ordinary user authentication and requires no user interaction, but the impact is limited to low confidentiality and integrity loss for same-owner brokered artifact objects across orgs.

## Recommended Remediation

Include the authenticated org in brokered artifact object namespaces and response prefixes. For example, derive keys as `CRABBOX_ARTIFACTS_PREFIX/<org>/<owner>/<request-prefix>/<name>` and pass both `requestOwner(request)` and `requestOrg(request, env)` into the artifact broker. Treat `org` as a required namespace component for user-scoped artifact grants, with a deliberate compatibility plan only if existing deployments need legacy URLs to remain readable.

Add regression coverage that:

- creates upload grants for the same owner with two different authenticated org headers and proves the returned prefixes and keys differ;
- proves the response prefix includes `org`;
- verifies caller-controlled `request.prefix` and file names cannot collapse or remove the `org` namespace component.

## Validation

Validation method: source review, documentation boundary review, and local CVSS scoring.

Verified evidence:

- `docs/security.md:67-72` documents signed user tokens as carrying `owner`, `org`, and GitHub `login`.
- `docs/security.md:85-88` states that user tokens can mutate coordinator resources only for their own `owner`/`org` identity.
- `docs/security.md:254-257` documents brokered artifact publishing credentials as server-side signing material for short-lived upload/read URLs.
- `worker/src/coordinator-entry.ts:86-106` authenticates ordinary coordinator routes and forwards trusted auth context before `POST /v1/artifacts/uploads` reaches the fleet.
- `worker/src/auth.ts:30-39` defines user-token payloads with both `owner` and `org`; `worker/src/auth.ts:90-100` returns both fields after token verification.
- `worker/src/auth.ts:126-146` writes both values into trusted `x-crabbox-owner` and `x-crabbox-org` headers.
- `worker/src/fleet.ts:855-856` exposes `POST /v1/artifacts/uploads` as a user-scoped route.
- `worker/src/fleet.ts:9046-9050` calls `artifactUploadResponse(this.env, input, requestOwner(request))`, dropping `org`.
- `worker/src/http.ts:28-30` returns only `x-crabbox-owner` for `requestOwner`.
- `worker/src/usage.ts:75-83` has a separate `requestOrg` helper, and surrounding fleet code uses it for many owner/org-scoped decisions, but the artifact upload path does not.
- `worker/src/artifacts.ts:57-68` derives the upload response prefix from `config.prefix`, `owner`, and caller prefix.
- `worker/src/artifacts.ts:191-201` confirms the object prefix is only `configPrefix`, `owner`, and `requestPrefix`.
- `worker/src/artifacts.ts:223-228` returns the final public URL or signed read URL for the same derived key.
- `worker/src/oauth.ts:428-465` derives stable `owner` from the GitHub user email, while `worker/src/oauth.ts:467-495` separately validates the requested allowed org, so the same GitHub user can receive tokens with the same owner and different org values.
- `internal/cli/artifacts_publish.go:203-252` sends caller prefix and file metadata to the broker and trusts the returned grant key/URL for upload and publication.
- `internal/cli/artifacts_publish.go:255-272` makes omitted prefixes unique with PR, bundle, and timestamp, but it does not add org and does not protect explicit prefixes.
- `worker/test/fleet.test.ts:16289-16348` currently asserts a brokered artifact key shape of `qa/peter@example.com/pr-42/screenshots/after.png`, which encodes owner-only namespacing.

Counterevidence considered:

- Default hosted publishing creates a unique client-side prefix when `--prefix` is omitted, reducing accidental overwrite risk. That does not enforce org separation at the coordinator boundary, and explicit prefixes remain caller-controlled.
- The issue requires the same GitHub owner to be valid in more than one allowed org on the same coordinator. It is therefore narrower than arbitrary cross-owner access, but still crosses the documented owner/org resource boundary.
- No live S3/R2 PUT or GET was performed during verification. Confidence is based on the coordinator's key derivation and signed URL generation path.
target.author: coygeek
